title: Login redirect loop after SSO change
body:
We recently switched our company's SSO provider from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Since the switch, some users are getting stuck in an infinite redirect loop when trying to log into TaskFlow. They click login, get redirected to Microsoft, authenticate successfully, get redirected back to TaskFlow, and then immediately get sent back to Microsoft again. Not all users are affected — maybe 30% of our team. The rest can log in fine.

We've already verified that the Entra ID app registration is correct and matches our redirect URIs. The affected users can log into other apps that use the same SSO just fine.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — that's a frustrating issue, especially mid-migration.

To narrow this down: have you identified any pattern that distinguishes the affected ~30% from the users who can log in fine? Specifically:

- Were the affected users previously logged into TaskFlow (i.e., might they have stale session cookies from the Okta integration)?
- Do the affected users have any differences in their Entra ID accounts — e.g., their UPN or email format differs, they're in different groups, or they're subject to different Conditional Access policies?

Also, does clearing the browser cookies/cache for TaskFlow resolve the loop for affected users, even temporarily?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Good questions. So clearing cookies does seem to help sometimes — a few users reported that clearing cookies and quickly logging back in worked, and one person got in using an incognito window. But it's not consistent, sometimes the loop just comes back.

As for patterns, I've been trying to figure that out. I noticed that a lot of the affected users have email addresses with a '+' in them — we use plus addressing for some people like jane+taskflow@company.com. Not sure if that's 100% the pattern but it stood out to me. There are also a couple users who had their email addresses changed or aliased when we moved to Entra, like they had a different primary email in Okta than what Entra is sending now.

The users who work fine seem to just have straightforward email addresses that are the same in both systems. I haven't looked into Conditional Access differences but all our users should be in the same policies.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That plus-addressing detail is a really promising lead — `+` characters are notorious for causing encoding issues in redirects and token handling.

Can you check what claim TaskFlow uses to identify users from the SSO response? Specifically:

1. In TaskFlow's SSO/OIDC configuration, is user matching based on `email`, `preferred_username`, `sub`, or something else?
2. Can you grab the actual claims from a failing authentication attempt? (Most frameworks log the incoming token claims at debug level, or you can decode the `id_token` from the redirect URL before the loop restarts — even a quick base64 decode of the JWT payload would work.)

I'm trying to determine whether the `+` in the email is getting mangled (e.g., URL-decoded to a space) or whether Entra is sending a different claim value than what TaskFlow has stored from the Okta era.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I'm pretty sure TaskFlow matches on the email claim — I remember setting that up when we originally configured the Okta integration. I'd have to double-check the exact config field name but it's definitely email-based, not the sub or UPN.

As for grabbing the actual claims from a failing attempt, I haven't done that yet. I'm not super familiar with decoding JWTs but I can try. I did notice in the browser dev tools on one affected user's machine that there's a Set-Cookie header showing up on the redirect back from Microsoft, but then the login page just loads again and sends them back. Not sure if that's relevant. I'll see if I can get the debug logs turned up and grab the token claims, but it might take me a bit.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That Set-Cookie observation is actually very relevant — it suggests TaskFlow is receiving the token, trying to establish a session, but then failing user lookup and kicking the user back to login.

Before you wrestle with JWT decoding, can you check something quicker: pick one of the affected plus-addressed users (e.g., `jane+taskflow@company.com`) and look up their user record in TaskFlow's database or admin panel. What email address does TaskFlow have stored for them? I want to know if it matches exactly what Entra would be sending in the `email` claim.

Also — for one of the users whose email changed during the Okta-to-Entra migration, what was their old email in Okta vs. their current primary email in Entra?

This will tell us whether the redirect loop is a claim-mismatch problem (TaskFlow can't find the user, so it restarts auth) vs. an encoding problem with the `+` character specifically.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
OK so I just checked. For Jane, TaskFlow has her stored as jane+taskflow@company.com — that's what Okta was sending. But I'm pretty sure Entra is sending just jane@company.com as the email claim, since that's her primary email in Entra and the plus part was just an alias. So yeah, those don't match.

For one of the aliased users — Mike — he was mike.s@company.com in Okta but when we set up Entra his primary ended up being michael.smith@company.com. TaskFlow still has him as mike.s@company.com.

So it does look like a mismatch problem for both cases. Though I'm still a little confused about why clearing cookies sometimes helps temporarily if it's just a lookup issue. Maybe that's a separate thing.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Login redirect loop due to email claim mismatch after Okta-to-Entra ID migration","problem":"After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users are stuck in an infinite login redirect loop. Users authenticate successfully with Entra but TaskFlow cannot match them to an existing account because the email address in Entra's email claim differs from what TaskFlow has stored from the Okta era.","root_cause_hypothesis":"TaskFlow performs user lookup using the `email` claim from the OIDC token. Two categories of users have mismatches: (1) users with plus-addressed emails (e.g., TaskFlow stores `jane+taskflow@company.com` but Entra sends `jane@company.com` as the primary email), and (2) users whose primary email changed during migration (e.g., TaskFlow stores `mike.s@company.com` but Entra sends `michael.smith@company.com`). When lookup fails, TaskFlow does not establish a valid session and redirects back to the IdP, creating the loop.","reproduction_steps":["Identify a user whose email in TaskFlow's database differs from their primary email in Entra ID (e.g., a plus-addressed user or one whose email changed during migration)","Have that user attempt to log into TaskFlow via SSO","User authenticates successfully with Microsoft Entra ID","Entra redirects back to TaskFlow with an id_token containing the Entra primary email","TaskFlow attempts user lookup by email claim, finds no match, fails to create a session","TaskFlow redirects the user back to Entra to re-authenticate, creating an infinite loop"],"environment":"TaskFlow with OIDC/SSO integration, recently migrated from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. User matching is configured on the `email` claim. Affects ~30% of users — those with plus-addressed emails or emails that changed during migration.","severity":"high","impact":"~30% of the team is effectively locked out of TaskFlow with no reliable workaround. Clearing cookies works intermittently but the loop returns. This is a blocking issue for daily work for affected users.","recommended_fix":"Two-pronged approach: (1) Immediate: Update affected users' email addresses in TaskFlow's database to match what Entra ID sends in the email claim — run a reconciliation script comparing TaskFlow stored emails against Entra directory entries. (2) Longer-term: Modify TaskFlow's user lookup to either match on a stable identifier like the OIDC `sub` claim instead of email, or support email alias mapping so that multiple email addresses can resolve to the same user. Additionally, add explicit error handling for the 'user not found after successful SSO' case — instead of silently restarting the auth flow, display an error message like 'Your SSO account could not be matched to a TaskFlow user. Contact your administrator.'","proposed_test_case":"Create a test user in TaskFlow with email `testuser+alias@company.com`. Configure the test IdP to return `testuser@company.com` as the email claim. Attempt login and verify that (a) after the fix, the user is matched correctly and logged in, and (b) if no match is found, a clear error is shown instead of a redirect loop.","information_gaps":["Exact TaskFlow SSO configuration field name for email claim mapping (reporter said 'pretty sure' it's email-based but hasn't confirmed the config)","Whether the intermittent cookie-clearing success is from residual Okta sessions or another mechanism","Whether Conditional Access policies differ across user groups (reporter believes they're uniform but hasn't verified)"]}
